ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00001516 covers cooked rice broadly; no specific Wuchang or Japonica steamed rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked rice generically. No Wuchang-specific or Japonica-specific code exists in FoodEx2. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226355009 covers cooked rice as a substance. No Wuchang-specific entry exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of steamed Wuchang rice is approximately 200 g, equivalent to roughly one cup of cooked rice, as typically served in a Chinese restaurant or hawker setting in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 168878)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Wuchang rice is a premium short-grain Japonica variety grown in Heilongjiang, China. USDA SR Legacy short-grain cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878) is the closest approved source match. Values are applied as representative of cooked Japonica white rice. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a Wuchang-specific entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this staple.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice, short-grain, cooked (GI = 72)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value specific to Wuchang Japonica rice is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest approved analogous food is white short-grain cooked rice (GI = 72, glucose reference), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 57.18) / 100 ≈ 41.2, based on a 200 g serving containing 57.18 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (1 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Steamed Wuchang rice is very low in sodium (approximately 1 mg per 100 g) and is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided it is served without high-sodium sauces, gravies, or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and high glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 41)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed white Wuchang rice has a high GI of approximately 72 and a high glycemic load per standard 200 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response, and consider lower-GI rice varieties or mixed-grain alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed Wuchang rice is very low in saturated fat (0.05 g per 100 g) and contains negligible cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly carbohydrate base when consumed in moderate portions as part of a balanced diet low in saturated and trans fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wuchang rice (五常米饭, Wǔ Cháng Mǐ Fàn) is a premium short-grain Japonica white rice originating from Wuchang County in Heilongjiang Province, northeastern China. It is widely regarded as one of China's finest rice varieties, prized for its slightly sticky texture, mild sweetness, and fragrant aroma when freshly steamed. The rice is prepared by washing the grains and steaming or boiling them in water until fully cooked, yielding soft, glossy, cohesive grains. In Singapore, it is served as a plain steamed rice accompaniment in Chinese restaurants and casual dining establishments, including Hong Kong-style bing sutt (冰室) cafes such as Keming Bing Sat. Nutritionally, steamed Wuchang rice is a high-carbohydrate, low-fat, low-sodium staple providing approximately 130 kcal per 100 g, with 28.6 g carbohydrate, 2.4 g protein, and 0.2 g fat. Its glycemic index is high (GI ≈ 72), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, and is suitable for most dietary patterns when consumed in appropriate portions alongside protein and vegetables.
common_user_queries: Is Wuchang rice good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Wuchang rice and regular white rice?
五常米饭 升糖指数

confidence_reason: Wuchang rice is a named premium Japonica variety not individually listed in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy short-grain cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), which is the closest approved source. GI is estimated from the analogous short-grain white rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium because no exact Wuchang-specific nutrient or GI record exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168878 (white short-grain cooked rice) as no Wuchang-specific entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plain steamed rice per USDA source. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plain cooked white rice per USDA source. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero for cooked white rice per USDA source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero for cooked white rice per USDA source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plain cooked white rice per USDA source. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plain cooked white rice per USDA source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plain cooked white rice per USDA source. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on short-grain white cooked rice from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database; no Wuchang-specific GI measurement was found in approved sources. 4. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 57.18) / 100 ≈ 41.2, based on a 200 g serving. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168878. 6. This record was served at Keming Bing Sat 克茗冰室 - North Bridge Road, Singapore, at SGD 2.00 per portion. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required by G6.
